events.text: Patient is a 42-year-old Hispanic male who presented to the emergency department with high fever, severe fatigue, and petechial rash involving bilateral lower extremities.
At age 42, the patient was diagnosed with acute myeloid leukemia NOS, ICD-O morphology code 9861/3, primary site bone marrow C42.1.

At age 42, initial CBC showed marked leukocytosis with white blood cell count of 148,000/uL with 74% blasts, hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL, and platelet count of 18,000/uL. LDH was markedly elevated at 2,340 U/L. Uric acid was 11.2 mg/dL. Coagulation panel showed mildly prolonged PT at 15.2 seconds and fibrinogen of 142 mg/dL.

At age 42, due to extreme hyperleukocytosis the patient underwent emergent leukapheresis to reduce circulating blast burden, with post-procedure WBC decreasing to 62,000/uL. Hydroxyurea was initiated concurrently.

At age 42, bone marrow biopsy with aspirate showed a packed marrow (100% cellularity) with 88% myeloblasts. Blasts were large with abundant cytoplasm, irregular nuclear contours, and frequent Auer rods. Flow cytometry immunophenotype showed blasts positive for CD34, CD117, CD33, CD13, HLA-DR, and cytoplasmic MPO. CD56 was aberrantly expressed.

At age 42, next-generation sequencing revealed FLT3-ITD mutation with allelic ratio 0.94, NPM1 mutation (type D insertion), and no additional adverse mutations. DNMT3A, TP53, RUNX1, and ASXL1 were all wild-type.
At age 42, conventional cytogenetics showed a normal male karyotype, 46,XY. ELN 2022 risk stratification was intermediate based on co-mutated FLT3-ITD and NPM1 with normal karyotype.

At age 42, once the white blood cell count was controlled, induction chemotherapy was started with 7+3 consisting of cytarabine 200 mg/m2 continuous infusion for seven days and daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 on days one through three, with midostaurin 50 mg orally twice daily on days eight through twenty-one.

At age 42, on day two of induction the patient developed clinical tumor lysis syndrome with potassium 6.8 mEq/L, phosphorus 8.9 mg/dL, uric acid 13.6 mg/dL, and acute kidney injury with creatinine rising to 3.2 mg/dL, requiring transfer to the intensive care unit and initiation of continuous renal replacement therapy for 48 hours.

At age 42, the patient developed neutropenic fever on day ten with temperature 40.1 degrees Celsius. Chest CT showed bilateral ground-glass opacities and bronchoalveolar lavage was positive for Pneumocystis jirovecii, treated with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
At age 42, day 14 bone marrow biopsy showed a hypocellular marrow (less than 5% cellularity) with rare residual blasts estimated at less than 2%, indicating an excellent early response.
At age 42, prolonged pancytopenia during induction resulted in grade 3 mucositis and Clostridium difficile colitis requiring oral vancomycin therapy.

At age 42, day 28 bone marrow biopsy demonstrated complete remission with 1.6% blasts by morphology. Multiparameter flow cytometry MRD was negative at a sensitivity of 0.01%. NPM1 mutation transcript level by RT-qPCR was reduced by 3.8 log from baseline.

At age 42, the patient received consolidation with four cycles of high-dose cytarabine 3 g/m2 every twelve hours on days one, three, and five, with midostaurin 50 mg twice daily on days eight through twenty-one of each cycle.

At age 42, given intermediate ELN risk with NPM1 co-mutation and achievement of MRD-negative complete remission, the multidisciplinary tumor board recommended consolidation chemotherapy with midostaurin maintenance rather than allogeneic transplant in first remission, with close MRD surveillance.
At age 42, following completion of four consolidation cycles, the patient was started on midostaurin maintenance 50 mg orally twice daily, planned for twelve cycles.
At age 42, during consolidation cycle three the patient developed grade 2 maculopapular rash attributed to cytarabine, managed with topical corticosteroids and antihistamines.

At age 43, at twelve months from diagnosis the patient remained in complete molecular remission with undetectable NPM1 mutation transcript and negative FLT3-ITD by PCR. MRD by flow cytometry remained negative on serial assessments.
At age 43, surveillance PET-CT showed no FDG-avid disease and no extramedullary leukemia. Spleen size was within normal limits.

At age 43, bone marrow biopsy at eighteen months from diagnosis continued to show complete morphologic remission with less than 1% blasts and trilineage hematopoiesis. Flow cytometry MRD remained negative and NPM1 transcript was undetectable by RT-qPCR, consistent with sustained deep molecular response.

CLINICAL PROGRESS NOTE

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
At age 42, initial CBC showed marked leukocytosis with white blood cell count of 148,000/uL with 74% blasts, hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL, and platelet count of 18,000/uL. LDH was markedly elevated at 2,340 U/L. Uric acid was 11.2 mg/dL. Coagulation panel showed mildly prolonged PT at 15.2 seconds and fibrinogen of 142 mg/dL.

The patient was seen in clinic today for ongoing oncologic management.

At age 42, next-generation sequencing revealed FLT3-ITD mutation with allelic ratio 0.94, NPM1 mutation (type D insertion), and no additional adverse mutations. DNMT3A, TP53, RUNX1, and ASXL1 were all wild-type.

FINDINGS:
At age 43, surveillance PET-CT showed no FDG-avid disease and no extramedullary leukemia. Spleen size was within normal limits.

Please correlate clinically.

CLINICAL PROGRESS NOTE

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
At age 43, bone marrow biopsy at eighteen months from diagnosis continued to show complete morphologic remission with less than 1% blasts and trilineage hematopoiesis. Flow cytometry MRD remained negative and NPM1 transcript was undetectable by RT-qPCR, consistent with sustained deep molecular response.

The patient was seen in clinic today for ongoing oncologic management.

scenario_blurb: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with FLT3-ITD mutation. ELN intermediate/adverse risk. Induction with 7+3 plus midostaurin. Some achieving CR, some refractory. Include cytogenetics (normal karyotype or complex), blast percentage monitoring, allogeneic stem cell transplant in first remission for eligible patients.
